statement: Guðrún Hafsteinsdóttir, speaking for Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party) on 9 March 2026, argued that the referendum proposal is not an innocent or non-binding exercise but a political decision to put Iceland back on the EU accession track. She criticised the referendum question as insufficient because it asks the public to authorise negotiations before the government has disclosed its specific negotiating objectives or conditions. She drew attention to the 2009 precedent where Alþingi voted to apply for EU membership with a promise of a final referendum on any accession agreement, contrasting it with the current approach of a non-binding advisory referendum. She stated: 'Ef ríkisstjórnin vill umboð frá þjóðinni til að fara í þessa vegferð þá er lágmark, algjört lágmark, að hún upplýsi þjóðina' (If the government wants a mandate from the nation for this journey, then the absolute minimum is that it informs the nation).
source_name: Alþingi — Guðrún Hafsteinsdóttir, þingmaður Sjálfstæðisflokksins (ræða, 157. löggjafarþing)
caveats: This is an opposition speech from the Independence Party, which has historically been EU-sceptic despite not having an official party position against EU membership. The criticism about lacking negotiating objectives reflects a legitimate procedural concern but may also serve as a strategic argument against the referendum itself.

related_entries.statement: The coalition government formed in late 2024, comprising Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), agreed in their coalition agreement to hold a referendum on whether to resume EU accession negotiations by 2027. The referendum was subsequently scheduled for 29 August 2026. The question asks whether Iceland should resume accession negotiations with the EU — not whether to join. This is the first Icelandic government since Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir's government (2009–2013) to actively pursue EU-related policy. Prime Minister Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin) and Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir (Viðreisn) have been the primary government figures advancing the referendum. Previous governments, including those led by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson (2013–2016) and Katrín Jakobsdóttir (2017–2024), did not pursue EU accession despite intermittent public calls for a referendum.

Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir presented a parliamentary resolution (stjórnartillaga til þingsályktunar) on 9 March 2026 proposing a referendum on 29 August 2026 with the question: 'Á að halda áfram viðræðum um aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu?' (Should negotiations on Iceland's EU accession continue?). In her flutningsræða (sponsor's speech), she framed the referendum as a sovereignty issue — arguing that allowing the nation to decide is itself an exercise of sovereignty. She drew parallels with three historical decisions (NATO membership, EFTA accession, EEA agreement) that were also contentious at the time but are now widely regarded as beneficial. She stated that Iceland's 2009 EU application remains valid, confirmed by the EU itself, and that negotiations could resume 'around the turn of the year' if the public votes yes. She explicitly pledged: 'Ég mun aldrei skrifa undir samning sem tryggir ekki okkar markmið og okkar yfirráð' (I will never sign a deal that does not secure our goals and our sovereignty) — specifically regarding fisheries. She cited precedents for special arrangements: Nordic agricultural exemptions (Sweden, Finland) and property purchase restrictions (Denmark, Malta).

Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson, leader of Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party), delivered an opposition speech on 9 March 2026 accusing the government of conducting 'baktjaldamakk' (behind-the-scenes machinations) with the EU. He argued that the government's plans were first revealed through foreign media, not to Alþingi or the utanríkismálanefnd (foreign affairs committee). He claimed the summer referendum date was deliberately chosen to minimise public debate, and that the referendum question is misleading because EU accession negotiations require the applicant country to immediately begin adapting its laws and institutions — it is not simply 'looking at what's on offer'. He referenced the period 2009–2013 when EU representatives themselves tried to explain to Icelandic politicians that there is no such thing as exploratory negotiations without commitment to join. He characterised the government as having 'given up' on its domestic economic programme and using the EU referendum as a distraction.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Íslenska þjóðin frétti fyrst af ESB-áformum ríkisstjórnarinnar í erlendum fjölmiðlum sem höfðu upplýsingarnar frá heimildarmönnum í Brussel, ekki frá ríkisstjórninni sjálfri.

Þingsályktunartillaga ríkisstjórnarinnar snýst um fullt forræði yfir auðlindum þjóðarinnar, en í tillögunni er talað um "forræði" en ekki "fullt forræði". Forsætisráðherra var ekki tilbúinn í dag að lýsa því yfir að fullt forræði væri skilyrði.
